acceptance_targets: [ch16_craft_01] Count every word in the notification text (lines 19-21). The total must exactly match the number stated by the narrator in all four instances (lines 33, 57, 167, 169). All four narrator references must cite the same number.

[ch16_prose_01] The construction 'the way [X] [verb/clause]' appears no more than 4 times in the revised chapter. Each surviving instance connects to a comparison that cannot be as effectively rendered in another syntactic form. The paragraph at line 83 contains zero instances of this construction.

[ch16_prose_02] No single paragraph contains more than 2 em-dash appositional constructions. The chapter's total count of em-dash asides is 18 or fewer. At least 3 paragraphs in the second half of the chapter (post-section-break at line 81) contain zero em-dash asides.

[ch16_craft_02] Any specific month-count in the chapter is consistent with the interval between mid-March (classification established) and early June (chapter setting). The stated duration should fall between 2.5 and 3 months, or use non-specific phrasing.

[ch16_craft_03] At least one passage in the revised chapter renders Dan's physical awareness of Mira as desire — located in the body (gut, skin, breath, heat, muscle tension), not in observation or metaphor. The moment may be brief or suppressed but must be sensorially present before any intellectual processing overrides it.

[ch16_prose_03] The 'Not X. Not Y. [But/Just] Z.' negative-definition construction appears no more than twice in the revised chapter, separated by at least 40 lines. The final instance appears in the chapter's closing movement (after line 160).

[ch16_prose_04] No more than 2 passages use a parallel catalogue of 4+ items. The longest catalogue appears in the chapter's final movement (after line 160). The three 'table where' anaphora at line 105 is reduced to two.

[ch16_prose_05] At least 3 of the chapter's 5 longest paragraphs contain a sentence of 15 words or fewer that delivers emotional or sensory content (not a transitional 'He stood up'). No stretch of 150+ consecutive words proceeds without a sentence under 20 words.

[ch16_award_01] At least 2 of the chapter's emotional-processing passages (currently at lines 27, 65, 119, 124-125) include a specific bodily sensation — a temperature, a muscle state, a change in breathing, a texture felt against skin — that precedes or accompanies the intellectual/metaphorical processing. The sensation must be rendered with the same specificity the chapter already applies to objects (e.g., the salt shaker's pale circle, the counter edge pressing into the small of his back).

findings.acceptance_test: Count every word in the notification text (lines 19-21). The total must exactly match the number stated by the narrator in all four instances (lines 33, 57, 167, 169). All four narrator references must cite the same number.
findings.evidence: The notification rendered at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:19-21 contains approximately 64 space-separated words across four sentences. The chapter describes it as 'forty-three words' at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:33 ('in the space of forty-three words'), :57 ('a forty-three-word notification'), :167 ('forty-three words among thousands'), and :169 ('had taken the form of forty-three words on a Tuesday morning'). The count is deployed as a structural motif four times to emphasize the disproportion between the notification's brevity and its life-altering consequence.
findings.problem: Internal word-count inconsistency. The rendered notification text is approximately 64 words; the narrator calls it 'forty-three words' four times across the chapter. Because the exact count is used as a recurring motif — emphasizing how absurdly few words it took to reorganize their lives — the error is structurally prominent and invites the reader to count. A discrepancy of 21 words between the stated and actual count undermines the device and signals revision-tracking failure (the spine originally planned 'fourteen words'; the notification was expanded but the motif count was never reconciled with the rendered text).
findings.rewrite_direction: Either (a) trim the notification at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:19-21 to exactly 43 words while preserving the key institutional phrases ('infrastructure classification discontinued,' 'standard dissolution protocols now available,' 'Bilateral Node K-4471,' 'Kessler/Mehta'), or (b) recount the notification and update all four references at lines 33, 57, 167, and 169 to the actual word count. Option (a) is preferred — the shorter the notification, the sharper the anticlimax. Consider compressing the first sentence (the preamble about the dispute) and the third sentence (the resource-release clause), which carry the least narrative-critical content.

findings.acceptance_test: The construction 'the way [X] [verb/clause]' appears no more than 4 times in the revised chapter. Each surviving instance connects to a comparison that cannot be as effectively rendered in another syntactic form. The paragraph at line 83 contains zero instances of this construction.
findings.evidence: The comparative construction 'the way [X] [verb/clause]' appears at minimum 12 times: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:11 ('the way he used to skim corporate newsletters'), :13 ('the way you know a sound in your apartment is wrong'), :23 ('the way he used to read contracts'), :37 ('the way every sound carried'), :65 ('the way weather settles into a landscape'), :79 ('the way every flaw in a shared apartment eventually becomes invisible'), :83 ('the way she held her phone' and 'the way she'd read the infrastructure classification' and 'the way she'd read the denial'), :95 ('the specific way a child breathes'), :113 ('the way June afternoons do'), :121 ('the way it fixed when she was processing').
findings.problem: The 'the way [X]' comparative is the chapter's most over-deployed sentence connector, appearing 12+ times in 186 lines. At this density it has crossed from Dan's voice into involuntary tic, forcing every analogy and comparison through the same syntactic scaffold. The style bible explicitly warns against prose constructions appearing more than twice in a chapter ('Once is a choice; twice is a coincidence; three times is a tic'). At twelve instances, the pattern flattens passages that should feel individually shaped.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'the way [X]' to no more than 4 instances. Prioritize keeping it where the comparative structure does irreplaceable work — e.g., snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:13 ('the way you know a sound in your apartment is wrong' earns its structure through the extended acoustic metaphor that follows). For the 8+ instances to revise, vary the approach by line: line 11 — convert to direct metaphor or simile; line 37 — recast as statement about the apartment's acoustics rather than comparison; line 65 — use an image rather than an abstract comparison; line 79 — deliver the observation through physical detail instead of generalization; line 83 (three instances in one paragraph) — this paragraph is the worst offender and needs the most varied reconstruction; line 95 — convert to direct description of the breathing; line 121 — recast as observation of Mira's behavior.

findings.acceptance_test: No single paragraph contains more than 2 em-dash appositional constructions. The chapter's total count of em-dash asides is 18 or fewer. At least 3 paragraphs in the second half of the chapter (post-section-break at line 81) contain zero em-dash asides.
findings.evidence: Em-dash appositional asides — pattern: '[noun or clause] — [elaborating or redefining phrase]' — appear 35+ times across the chapter. Dense clusters at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:27 (3 em-dash constructions in one paragraph: 'the designation that had turned...', 'the word you used for...', 'a pale circle in the grain like...'), :71 (3 constructions: 'she'd started needing them this year...', 'the worn cotton pants she slept in...', 'shoved up on top of her head like...'), :105 (4+ constructions), :169 (5+ constructions in a single paragraph), :183 (3+ constructions). Additional instances at lines 5, 11, 13, 29, 33, 37, 41, 45, 53, 63, 73, 79, 83, 93, 95, 109, 113, 115, 117, 119, 121, 123, 127, 137, 145, 153, 157, 159, 161.
findings.problem: The em-dash appositional aside is the chapter's overwhelmingly dominant syntactic move, appearing in nearly every paragraph and multiple times in the longest paragraphs. The result is a recursive-elaboration rhythm — statement, restatement, further restatement — that becomes the chapter's only mode. While the construction is consonant with Dan's circling, self-qualifying thought process, its sheer dominance (35+ instances, with some paragraphs containing 4-5) creates monotony that violates the style bible's instruction to avoid 'settling into any single rhythmic default.' The reader absorbs every paragraph through the same syntactic shape.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash appositional constructions by approximately 50%, targeting the densest paragraphs first. Priority revision targets: (1) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:169 — the chapter's climactic paragraph contains 5+ em-dash asides; reduce to 2, letting the accumulative list carry the weight without constant appositional interruption. (2) Line 71 — Mira's entrance has 3 parenthetical qualifications; let her physical description stand without editorial aside. (3) Lines 105 and 123-125 — Dan's meditation in the bedroom chains 4+ asides; convert at least 2 into new sentences or integrate into the main clause. (4) Lines 27-29 — the first emotional processing passage stacks parenthetical after parenthetical; select the strongest and cut the rest. For each removed aside, the options are: (a) make it a new sentence, (b) integrate it into the main clause, (c) delete it where the restatement adds nothing the first statement didn't convey.

findings.acceptance_test: Any specific month-count in the chapter is consistent with the interval between mid-March (classification established) and early June (chapter setting). The stated duration should fall between 2.5 and 3 months, or use non-specific phrasing.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:41 ('the woman he'd been sharing a two-bedroom apartment with for four months of enforced cohabitation') and :103 ('four months of our lives were organized around a thing we didn't understand'). Per the continuity sheet timeline, the infrastructure classification was established in mid-March (Chapter 3, 'Mid-March, about a week after filing'). Chapter 16 is set in 'Early June' (line 5). Mid-March to early June is approximately 2.5 months. Even dating from the divorce decision (late February, Chapter 1), the span is approximately 3-3.5 months.
findings.problem: Timeline inconsistency. Both Dan's narration and Mira's dialogue cite 'four months,' but the established timeline places the infrastructure classification at mid-March and the chapter in early June — approximately 2.5-3 months. Mira's version at line 103 is especially noticeable because she is characterized as analytically precise and is making a pointed rhetorical statement about the duration of their ordeal.
findings.rewrite_direction: Change both references at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:41 and :103 to a duration consistent with the established timeline. Options: (a) 'three months' or 'nearly three months' (most accurate for the classification period); (b) a vaguer phrasing like 'months of enforced cohabitation' at line 41 and 'months of our lives' at line 103 (avoids pinning a number); (c) if the author prefers to keep 'four months,' the chapter's date setting at line 5 must shift from 'Early June' to 'mid-July,' which would conflict with the continuity sheet and require downstream adjustments.

findings.acceptance_test: At least one passage in the revised chapter renders Dan's physical awareness of Mira as desire — located in the body (gut, skin, breath, heat, muscle tension), not in observation or metaphor. The moment may be brief or suppressed but must be sensorially present before any intellectual processing overrides it.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:73 registers physical proximity: 'close enough that he could smell her shampoo and the specific warmth of skin that had just been under hot water.' This is the chapter's only moment of sensory awareness of Mira's body. Line 169 explicitly names 'the almost-kiss in the car' and 'wanting was available again.' The style bible's intimacy_and_desire_policy: 'Dan and Mira's residual physical attraction is a narrative force, not background radiation. When the novel's relational pressure calls for it, render desire with the same specificity as any other emotional state — where in the body it lives, how it changes the air in a room.' The chapter removes the external barrier to desire and then does not render desire.
findings.problem: Timidity. The chapter establishes every precondition for rendered desire: the constraint is lifted, proximity is forced in the small kitchen, Dan smells her skin, the narrator names wanting as 'available again.' But line 73's sensory moment is immediately intellectualized into the infrastructure metaphor ('the only thing holding them in place now was whatever they decided') rather than allowed to register as desire in Dan's body. For a chapter that is precisely about the removal of barriers, the absence of any physical pull — even fleeting, even unwanted, even quickly suppressed — is a retreat from the emotional reality the premise establishes. The style bible's intimacy policy specifically warns against treating attraction as 'background radiation.'
findings.rewrite_direction: Render Dan's physical awareness of Mira as desire in at least one of two proximity moments: (1) Mira's entrance at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:71 (she appears in sleep clothes, damp-haired, in a small kitchen) — expand line 73's 'the specific warmth of skin' into a moment where Dan's body registers something before his narrating mind can convert it to metaphor; or (2) the post-yogurt-exchange moment at line 153 (Mira smiles, Dan watches) — add a physical register to what is currently only visual observation. The moment need not be overt; it can be one sentence — a catch in breathing, heat in the chest, the pull to close the six-foot gap — that is then overridden by the larger emotional confusion. But the body must speak before the intellect takes over.

findings.acceptance_test: The 'Not X. Not Y. [But/Just] Z.' negative-definition construction appears no more than twice in the revised chapter, separated by at least 40 lines. The final instance appears in the chapter's closing movement (after line 160).
findings.evidence: Negative-definition formula ('Not X. Not Y. [But/Just] Z.') at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:27 ('Not overturned. Not appealed. Not strategically dissolved...'), :119 ('Not released, because release implied an agent doing the releasing...'), :124 ('Not numbness.'), :169 ('Not optimized. Not classified. Not infrastructure. Just two people...'), :183 ('Not optimized. Not classified. Not infrastructure. Just two people and a walk...').
findings.problem: The chapter deploys a define-by-negation formula at 4-5 key emotional beats: Dan circles what he feels by listing what it isn't before arriving at what it is (or declining to arrive). While thematically apt — the chapter is about the removal of categories — the repetition across the chapter's most weighted passages makes those passages structurally interchangeable. Each emotional climax arrives through the same syntactic move, which dulls the final instance (line 169/183) where the device should land with maximum force.
findings.rewrite_direction: Retain the 'Not X. Not Y. Just Z.' pattern for at most 2 instances, spaced at least 40 lines apart. The strongest candidates are: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:27 (the first emotional processing of 'discontinued,' where the negation list establishes the device) and :183 (the final paragraph, where 'Not optimized. Not classified. Not infrastructure. Just two people' should be the payoff). For lines 119 and 124 — which are nearly adjacent — deliver the same emotional content through different means: direct statement, sensory metaphor, or physical action. Line 119's 'Not released' can be recast as a positive statement about what Dan does feel. Line 124's 'Not numbness' can be rendered through sensation rather than negation.

findings.acceptance_test: No more than 2 passages use a parallel catalogue of 4+ items. The longest catalogue appears in the chapter's final movement (after line 160). The three 'table where' anaphora at line 105 is reduced to two.
findings.evidence: Extended polysyndetic accumulation lists (4+ parallel items cataloguing what was classified/released) at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:27 ('their apartment into infrastructure, their marriage into a strategic asset, their bathroom schedule into a resource allocation, their son's cereal routine into a dependency'), :33 ('Mira had found the wall. Mira had mapped the wall. Mira had located a crack in the wall...'), :105 ('The table where she'd laid out printouts... The table where she'd sat at midnight... The table where Theo ate cereal'), :169 ('its apartment, its finances, its custody arrangement, its morning routine, its bathroom schedule, its refrigerator shelves, its child's drawing habit, its 2 a.m. conversations, its fights and its silences and its night on the couch'), :183 ('past the bodega on the corner and the dry cleaner and the brownstone').
findings.problem: The accumulative catalogue — listing specific items through parallel structure to convey the scope of what was classified or released — is the chapter's default device for generating emotional weight. At 5 instances, the early uses (lines 27, 33, 105) diminish the force of the climactic catalogue at line 169, which should be the chapter's most powerful passage. The reader has already absorbed this rhetorical move three times before it arrives at its intended peak.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reserve the full accumulative catalogue (4+ parallel items) for the chapter's climactic paragraph at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:169, where it carries the most emotional force. Compress earlier instances: at line 27, select the single most absurd reclassification ('their bathroom schedule into a resource allocation') and cut the four-item expansion to two. At line 33, compress the three 'Mira had [X] the wall' sentences into one. At line 105, reduce the three 'The table where' anaphora to two, selecting the two that contrast most sharply (midnight strategy vs. Theo's cereal). The line 183 catalogue is short and functions differently (sensory walk) and can remain.

findings.acceptance_test: At least 3 of the chapter's 5 longest paragraphs contain a sentence of 15 words or fewer that delivers emotional or sensory content (not a transitional 'He stood up'). No stretch of 150+ consecutive words proceeds without a sentence under 20 words.
findings.evidence: The chapter's interiority passages are built almost exclusively from long multi-clause sentences. Representative passages: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:5 (~120 words, one sentence), :11 (~80 words), :27 (~130 words), :33 (~150 words), :65 (~180 words), :105 (~130 words), :119 (~120 words), :169 (~220 words). Short sentences appear primarily as dialogue beats and transitions ('He put down the toast' at line 15, 'He answered' at line 43, 'He sat down' at line 131) but rarely within the chapter's reflective passages, where emotional weight is heaviest.
findings.problem: Rhythmic monotony. The chapter's reflective and emotional passages are built almost exclusively from long recursive sentences (80-220 words), with short declarative sentences confined to transitional moments. The style bible requires 'Short declarative sentences for comic timing, for shock, and for moments of clarity' alongside the long sentences, and warns against 'settling into any single rhythmic default — if three consecutive paragraphs use the same sentence structure, one of them is wrong.' The chapter's second half (post-section-break) is particularly uniform in sentence length, creating a wall of recursive elaboration that numbs rather than accumulates.
findings.rewrite_direction: Introduce 5-8 short declarative sentences (under 15 words) into the chapter's reflective passages — not as transitions but as emotional punctuation that interrupts and recharges the long-sentence rhythm. Target locations: (1) after snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:27's long 'discontinued' meditation — insert a short sentence that lands the emotional impact physically before the paragraph continues. (2) Within line 65's long paragraph about opacity — break the recursive chain with a sentence of blunt statement or physical action. (3) In the bedroom passage at lines 123-125 — interrupt the abstract meditation with a concrete bodily sensation in a short sentence. (4) Within line 169's climactic paragraph — the 220-word sentence needs at least one internal break where a short, simple sentence creates contrast. The short sentences should deliver emotional content, not merely transition between paragraphs.

findings.acceptance_test: At least 2 of the chapter's emotional-processing passages (currently at lines 27, 65, 119, 124-125) include a specific bodily sensation — a temperature, a muscle state, a change in breathing, a texture felt against skin — that precedes or accompanies the intellectual/metaphorical processing. The sensation must be rendered with the same specificity the chapter already applies to objects (e.g., the salt shaker's pale circle, the counter edge pressing into the small of his back).
findings.evidence: The chapter's emotional peaks are processed through metaphor and analogy rather than bodily sensation: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:27 ('The word did something specific to Dan's chest, something he had to stand still for a moment to locate' — names a location but provides no sensation), :65 ('a certainty that settled into his chest the way weather settles into a landscape' — the simile converts feeling into geography), :119 ('he felt the way he imagined something felt when the container it had been stored in was removed' — double remove: imagining what an imagined container-object would feel), :124-125 ('he could not locate his own edges. He didn't know what shape he was' — spatial metaphors for the body that never arrive at the body). Dan's style-bible stress tell — fixating on irrelevant physical details while his world falls apart — is well-deployed for setting (peanut butter, salt shaker, counter edge) but underdeployed for emotional processing.
findings.problem: Cognitive emotional processing at award level. Dan's feelings are consistently intellectualized through metaphor, analogy, and abstract spatial concepts rather than rendered as bodily sensation. The chapter has strong setting-level sensory detail (touch, sound, objects) but processes its key emotional moments — the ones that should hit the reader hardest — through an insulating layer of comparison and conceptualization. At shortlist level, the reader needs to feel the anticlimax in Dan's body, not only follow his mind's increasingly elaborate analogies for it. The gap between the chapter's superb sensory rendering of objects (the salt shaker, the bedspread, the coffee pot) and its abstract rendering of emotion is the primary barrier to award-level impact.
findings.rewrite_direction: In at least 2 of the 4 emotional-processing passages (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_16.md:27, :65, :119, :124-125), replace or supplement the metaphorical/cognitive processing with bodily sensation that precedes the intellectual gloss. Line 27: 'something specific to Dan's chest' names a body location — give it a physical quality (weight, tightness, heat, cold, a specific kind of ache). Line 124-125: 'could not locate his own edges' is a spatial metaphor about the body that never arrives at the body itself — what happens to Dan's posture on the bed, what do his hands do, what does the cotton bedspread feel like against palms that suddenly have nothing to grip? The chapter's strength is its capacity for specific physical detail (it renders toast and salt shakers beautifully); the revision needs to apply that same specificity to the body's experience of emotion, not just the mind's processing of it.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's risk zone is the gap between dignity and absurdity. The characters are adults with professional histories, a child, a mortgage — people with legitimate self-respect — who have been reduced to warm bodies in an optimization framework they cannot understand. The comedy must not protect them from the humiliation; the humiliation must not neutralize the comedy. Render both registers at full force, often in the same scene. The temptation will be to soften the comedy into gentle satire or to darken the humiliation into dystopian grimness — resist both. The novel's territory is the painful and the funny coexisting without one defusing the other. Commit to the specific physical embarrassment of being professionally irrelevant. Commit to the specific tenderness of parenting under surveillance. Commit to the specific grief of a marriage that might still be alive inside a system that has classified it as infrastructure. When in doubt, choose the version that makes you wince and then laugh, or laugh and then wince — both are correct.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene requires the reader to sit with discomfort — economic shame, marital cruelty, parental failure, institutional humiliation, the specific indignity of being professionally redundant — render it with full specificity. 'They struggled with the situation' is not a sentence that belongs in this novel. Name the thing. Show the body. Let the reader be uncomfortable.
aesthetic_risk_policy.intimacy_and_desire_policy: Dan and Mira's residual physical attraction is a narrative force, not background radiation. When the novel's relational pressure calls for it, render desire with the same specificity as any other emotional state — where in the body it lives, how it changes the air in a room, the precise quality of proximity when two people who know each other's bodies are trying to pretend they don't. The almost-kiss in Chapter 7 should be as fully rendered as any confrontation scene.
aesthetic_risk_policy.register_commitment: This is a dark comedy, not a cautionary tale, not a dystopia, not a satire with a thesis. The novel should not argue about AI. It should render what it feels like to be a body in a world where bodies are overhead. The comic register is not a veneer over a serious message — it IS the message. A scene that is genuinely funny about genuine suffering is doing more literary work than a scene that is solemnly observant about the same material.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the default for all spoken English in this novel. Uncontracted forms should be reserved for deliberate emphasis, Lena Morse's institutional register, or moments where a character is being carefully precise. If a line of dialogue uses 'do not' instead of 'don't' or 'I am' instead of 'I'm,' there should be a reason audible in the scene's pressure.
dialogue_rules.interruption_and_overlap_policy: Use interruptions, sentence fragments, and crosstalk when scene pressure warrants. Dan and Mira in particular should interrupt each other — they know each other's arguments before the sentences end. But every interruption must be motivated; do not scatter dashes for rhythmic texture alone.
dialogue_rules.leverage_shift_guidance: Every exchange of more than three lines should shift the power balance between speakers. This doesn't require overt conflict — a silence, a deflection, a piece of information withheld or accidentally revealed, a joke that lands wrong, a question left unanswered can all shift leverage. The dialogue must be going somewhere, not circling.
dialogue_rules.silence_as_dialogue: Silence, non-answers, and topic changes are valid dialogue moves and often more revealing than speech. When a character doesn't respond to something, the reader should feel the weight of what wasn't said. Render these moments through physical action or observed stillness, not by narrating that the character chose not to speak.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should share the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (single-scene compression vs. multi-scene chapters), dialogue density (some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are nearly silent), interiority depth (some chapters live mostly in the POV character's head, others are externally rendered), pacing (some chapters cover hours, others cover weeks in montage), and tonal register (comic chapters adjacent to devastating ones). The contrast between chapters is itself a narrative tool — use it to create whiplash, irony, and accumulating emotional force.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, precise, and register-sensitive. The prose absorbs the vocabulary of its setting: corporate language, tech terminology, bureaucratic formulas — but always filtered through the POV character's relationship to that language. Dan uses corporate words reflexively and with slight discomfort. Mira uses strategic vocabulary as native speech. The narrator should not editorialize on the language; let the words sit in context and let the reader hear the dissonance. Avoid: prestige-literary abstractions that haven't been earned by sensory grounding, and journalistic explainer-prose that breaks character to tell the reader about the world.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, rotating between Dan and Mira as primary POV characters. The camera stays tight to the current POV character's perceptions and vocabulary — what Dan notices is different from what Mira notices. In shared-POV chapters, the rotation should be clear (section breaks or sustained passages in one POV before shifting) rather than head-hopping within a scene.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied and responsive to scene pressure. Short declarative sentences for comic timing, for shock, and for moments of clarity. Longer, more syntactically complex sentences for interiority, sensory immersion, and emotional complexity. The rhythm should accelerate during confrontation and slow during reflection. Avoid settling into any single rhythmic default — if three consecutive paragraphs use the same sentence structure, one of them is wrong.
